author: śrīla saccidānanda bhaktibinoda ṭhākura
verses:
rasika⋅nāgarī⋅, gaṇa⋅śiromaṇi,
kṛṣṇaprema⋅sarahaṁsī
vṛṣabhānurāja⋅, śuddhakalpavallī,
sarvalakṣmīgaṇa⋅aṁśī

rakta paṭṭavastra, nitamba⋅upari,
kṣudra ghaṇṭi dule tā’ya
kucayugopari, duli’ muktāmālā,
cittahārī śobhā pāya

sarasijavara⋅, karṇikā⋅samāna,
atiśaya kāntimatī
kaiśora⋅amṛta⋅, tāruṇya⋅karpūra⋅,
miśrasmitādharā satī

vanānte āgata, vrajapati⋅suta,
parama cañcalavare
heri’ śaṅkākula, nayana⋅bhaṅgite,
ādarete stava kare

vrajera mahilā⋅, gaṇera parāṇa,
jaśomatī⋅priyapātrī
lalita⋅lalitā⋅, snehete praphulla⋅,
śarīrā lalitagātrī

viśākhāra sane, vanaphula tuli’,
gā̐the vaijayantī mālā
sakala⋅śreyasī, kṛṣṇa⋅vakṣaḥsthitā,
parama preyasī bālā

snigdha veṇurave, drutagati jāi’,
kuñje pe’ye naṭavare
hasita⋅nayanī, namramukhī satī,
karṇa kaṇḍūyana kare

sparśiyā kamala, vāyu suśītala,
kare jabe kuṇḍanīra
nidāghe tathāya, nijagaṇa⋅saha,
tuṣaya gokula⋅vīra

bhakativinoda, rūpa⋅raghunāthe,
kahaye caraṇa dhari’
hena rādhā⋅dāsya, sudhīra⋅sampad,
kabe dibe kṛpā kari’
en_translation:
The expert lover and crest-jewel of all, She is a swan in the lake of Kṛṣṇa's love. She is a pure desire-creeper from King Vṛṣabhānu and a part of all the goddesses of fortune.

Upon her hips are red silk garments, and tiny bells swing there. Upon her breasts, a swinging pearl necklace creates a heart-stealing beauty.

She is exceedingly radiant, like the beautiful heart of a lotus flower—a chaste lady with a sweet, gentle smile, infused with the nectar of youth.

When the son of the King of Vraja, the supremely restless lover, arrives at the forest edge, She sees Him with anxious glances and affectionately offers praise.

She is the very life of the women of Vraja and dear to Yaśodā. She is delicate and charming, her graceful body blooming with affection.

With Viśākhā, she picks forest flowers and weaves a Vaijayantī garland. She is the most auspicious of all, residing on Kṛṣṇa’s chest—the supremely beloved young girl.

Hearing the soft sound of the flute, she goes quickly to the grove and finds the best of dancers; with smiling eyes, the chaste woman with a humble face scratches her ear.

When the cool breeze touching the lotuses makes ripples in the lake during the summer, there she pleases the hero of Gokula along with her companions.

Bhaktivinoda says, holding the feet of Rūpa and Raghunātha: “When will you mercifully bestow upon me such service to Rādhā, that peaceful treasure?”
